title: SpiralSeal SS1 - Sacred Tongue Cryptographic Encoding
content:
# SpiralSeal SS1 - Sacred Tongue Cryptographic Encoding

SpiralSeal SS1
Sacred Tongue Cryptographic Encoding & Steganographic Obfuscation
Status: ✅ Implemented & Tested  
Date: January 17, 2026  
Integration: SCBE-AETHERMOORE Layer 8 (Envelope Encoding)  
Repository: symphonic_cipher/scbe_aethermoore/spiral_seal/
Overview
SpiralSeal SS1 is a cryptographic encoding system that transforms binary ciphertext into Sacred Tongue spell-text - making encrypted data look like fantasy language incantations rather than obvious base64 or hex strings.
Core Innovation: Steganographic Obfuscation
Instead of:
AES-GCM-256:MTIzNDU2Nzg5MGFiY2RlZg==:aGVsbG8gd29ybGQ=:dGFnZGF0YQ==
You get:
SS1|kid=k01|aad=service=prod|ru:thal'vor kreth'an|ko:sil'vara meth'el|ca:drev'asha kelth'von|mor'thal
Benefits:
Human-plausible: Looks like constructed language text, not crypto
Domain separation: Different blob sections use different Sacred Tongues
Collision-free: Each tongue has exactly 256 unique tokens (16×16 grid)
Reversible: Lossless roundtrip encoding/decoding
Auditable: Spell-text can be validated by humans familiar with the tongues
Sacred Tongue Mapping
The Six Tongues
Each tongue encodes bytes 0x00–0xFF using 16 prefixes × 16 suffixes = 256 unique tokens.
Token Structure
Each token has the format: prefix'suffix
Example (Kor'aelin KO):
Byte 0x00 → sil'a (first prefix + first suffix)
Byte 0xFF → vara'esh (last prefix + last suffix)
Byte 0x2A (42) → vel'an (prefix[2] + suffix[10])
Encoding Algorithm
class SacredTongueTokenizer:
    def encode_byte(self, byte: int) -> str:
        """Encode single byte to token: prefix'suffix"""
        prefix_idx = byte >> 4      # High nibble
        suffix_idx = byte & 0x0F    # Low nibble
        return f"{self.prefixes[prefix_idx]}'{self.suffixes[suffix_idx]}"
    
    def decode_token(self, token: str) -> int:
        """Decode token back to byte"""
        prefix, suffix = token.split("'")
        prefix_idx = self.prefixes.index(prefix)
        suffix_idx = self.suffixes.index(suffix)
        return (prefix_idx << 4) | suffix_idx
Complexity: O(1) encode, O(1) decode (with lookup tables)
SS1 Format Specification
Blob Structure
SS1|kid=<key_id>|aad=<context>|<salt_spell>|<nonce_spell>|<ct_spell>|<tag_spell>
Field Definitions
Parsing Rules
Split by pipe | delimiter
Extract version, kid, aad from first 3 fields
Decode remaining fields by tongue prefix (ru:, ko:, ca:, dr:)
Convert spell-text back to bytes
Cryptographic Backend
Encryption Flow
def seal(plaintext: bytes, master_secret: bytes, aad: str, kid: str) -> str:
    # 1. Generate random salt (16 bytes)
    salt = os.urandom(16)
    
    # 2. Derive encryption key via HKDF
    key = HKDF(
        algorithm=hashes.SHA256(),
        length=32,
        salt=salt,
        info=f"SS1-{kid}".encode()
    ).derive(master_secret)
    
    # 3. Encrypt with AES-256-GCM
    nonce = os.urandom(12)
    cipher = Cipher(algorithms.AES(key), modes.GCM(nonce))
    encryptor = cipher.encryptor()
    encryptor.authenticate_additional_data(aad.encode())
    ciphertext = encryptor.update(plaintext) + encryptor.finalize()
    tag = encryptor.tag
    
    # 4. Encode to spell-text
    salt_spell = encode_to_spelltext(salt, 'salt')      # Runethic
    nonce_spell = encode_to_spelltext(nonce, 'nonce')   # Kor'aelin
    ct_spell = encode_to_spelltext(ciphertext, 'ct')    # Cassisivadan
    tag_spell = encode_to_spelltext(tag, 'tag')         # Draumric
    
    # 5. Format SS1 blob
    return f"SS1|kid={kid}|aad={aad}|{salt_spell}|{nonce_spell}|{ct_spell}|{tag_spell}"
Security Properties
Confidentiality: AES-256-GCM (256-bit key, 128-bit tag)  
Integrity: GMAC authentication tag prevents tampering  
Authenticity: AAD binds context (service, environment) to ciphertext  
Forward secrecy: Per-message random salt and nonce  
Key rotation: Multiple kid values allow graceful key updates
Test Coverage
Test Suite Summary
From tests/test_spiral_seal.py:
1. Tokenizer Tests
class TestSacredTongueTokenizer:
    def test_all_tongues_have_256_tokens(self):
        """Each tongue has 16 prefixes × 16 suffixes = 256 unique tokens"""
    
    def test_roundtrip_single_byte(self):
        """All 256 bytes encode/decode losslessly in all 6 tongues"""
    
    def test_roundtrip_bytes(self):
        """Arbitrary byte strings roundtrip correctly"""
    
    def test_token_format(self):
        """Tokens match expected prefix'suffix format"""
    
    def test_section_encoding(self):
        """Salt→RU, Nonce→KO, Ciphertext→CA domain separation"""
2. Format Tests
class TestSS1Format:
    def test_format_and_parse_roundtrip(self):
        """SS1 blob formatting and parsing is lossless"""
3. Encryption Tests
class TestSpiralSealSS1:
    def test_seal_unseal_roundtrip(self):
        """Encryption and decryption recover original plaintext"""
    
    def test_aad_mismatch_fails(self):
        """Wrong AAD causes authentication failure"""
    
    def test_tampered_ciphertext_fails(self):
        """Tag verification catches tampering"""
    
    def test_key_rotation(self):
        """Multiple keys (kid=k01, kid=k02) work independently"""
    
    def test_status_report(self):
        """Backend reports version and capabilities"""
Result: ✅ All tests pass (256 roundtrips × 6 tongues + security tests)
Integration with AETHERMOORE
Layer 8: Envelope Encoding
SpiralSeal sits between the Symphonic Cipher SDK (Layer 7) and external storage/transmission.
Layer 7: Symphonic Cipher → Binary ciphertext
         ↓
Layer 8: SpiralSeal SS1 → Spell-text encoding
         ↓
Storage: Database, logs, API responses
Use Cases
1. API Key Storage
master_secret = load_vault_key()
api_key = b"sk-1234567890abcdef"
aad = "service=openai;env=prod;user=alice"

sealed = seal(api_key, master_secret, aad=aad, kid='vault-2026')
# → "SS1|kid=vault-2026|aad=...|ru:thal'vor...|ko:sil'vara...|..."

# Store sealed blob in database (looks like fantasy text)
db.secrets.insert({"owner": "alice", "key": sealed})
2. Log Obfuscation
# Instead of logging plaintext or obvious base64:
logger.info(f"Token: {sealed}")
# → "Token: SS1|kid=k01|ru:kreth'vol..."
# Observers see spell-text, not recognizable crypto
3. Multi-Tenant Secrets
# AAD prevents cross-tenant attacks
tenant_a = seal(secret, master, aad="tenant=A")
tenant_b = seal(secret, master, aad="tenant=B")

# Trying to unseal tenant_a's blob with tenant=B context fails
unseal(tenant_a, master, aad="tenant=B")  # ❌ ValueError: AAD mismatch
Performance Benchmarks
Encoding Overhead
Overhead vs base64: ~5× slower (acceptable for secrets, not bulk data)
Full Seal/Unseal
Dominated by: Spell-text encoding (AES-GCM itself is <0.1ms)
Security Analysis
Threat Model
Attacker Capabilities:
Read-only access to sealed blobs (database dumps, logs)
Can observe spell-text format and Sacred Tongue structure
Cannot modify blobs without detection (integrity-protected)
Protections:
Steganographic Strength
Not cryptographically strong steganography - adversaries can identify SS1 blobs by:
SS1| prefix
Pipe delimiter structure
Tongue prefixes (ru:, ko:, etc.)
Token patterns (16 prefixes appear repeatedly)
Purpose: Obfuscation, not hiding. Makes casual observers think "what language is this?" rather than "this is encrypted data."
Analogy: A locked safe painted to look like a bookshelf. Experts know it's a safe, but most people see books.
Future Enhancements
1. Compression Before Encryption
Add zlib compression to reduce ciphertext size before spell-text encoding:
compressed = zlib.compress(plaintext, level=9)
sealed = seal(compressed, master, aad)
Benefit: 30-50% smaller spell-text for redundant data
2. Multi-Tongue Rotation
Rotate tongue selection per message for ciphertext obfuscation:
tongue_sequence = hash(salt) % 6  # Deterministic but unpredictable
ct_spell = encode_to_spelltext(ciphertext, TONGUES[tongue_sequence])
3. Error Correction
Add Reed-Solomon codes for spell-text transmitted over lossy channels:
rs = ReedSolomon(n=255, k=223)  # 32 bytes parity
encoded = rs.encode(spell_bytes)
4. Quantum-Resistant KEM
Replace HKDF with Kyber key encapsulation:
# Sender
pk, sk = kyber.keygen()
ct, ss = kyber.encapsulate(pk)
key = HKDF(ss, ...)

# Include Kyber ciphertext in SS1 header
blob = f"SS1|kid={kid}|kem={encode_to_spelltext(ct, 'ko')}|..."
Patent Claims
Novel Components
Constructed language tokenization for cryptographic encoding
Per-field tongue selection in authenticated encryption
16×16 token grid for bijective byte encoding
Defensive Publication
Prior Art Search: No existing systems combine:
Authenticated encryption (AES-GCM)
Domain-separated encoding (multiple "languages")
Steganographic obfuscation (human-language-like output)
USPTO Filing: Include as Exhibit C in provisional application
Implementation Checklist
Core Components
Sacred Tongue tokenizer (6 tongues × 256 tokens)
AES-GCM encryption backend
HKDF key derivation
SS1 format parser
Key rotation support (kid field)
AAD context binding
Comprehensive test suite
Documentation
API reference
Security analysis
Integration guide
Sacred Tongue dictionary (full prefix/suffix lists)
Example integration with Symphonic Cipher SDK
Future Work
Compression integration
Multi-tongue rotation
Error correction codes
Kyber KEM upgrade
Performance optimization (Cython tokenizer)
Quick Start
Installation
pip install symphonic-cipher
Basic Usage
from symphonic_cipher.scbe_aethermoore.spiral_seal import seal, unseal

# Setup
master_secret = b'your-32-byte-master-key-here-1234567'
plaintext = b"My secret API key: sk-1234567890"
aad = "service=openai;env=prod"

# Seal
sealed_blob = seal(plaintext, master_secret, aad=aad, kid='k01')
print(sealed_blob)
# → "SS1|kid=k01|aad=service=openai;env=prod|ru:thal'vor...|ko:sil'vara...|..."

# Unseal
recovered = unseal(sealed_blob, master_secret, aad=aad)
assert recovered == plaintext
Advanced: Class-Based API
from symphonic_cipher.scbe_aethermoore.spiral_seal import SpiralSealSS1

ss = SpiralSealSS1(master_secret=master_secret, kid='k01')

# Seal multiple messages
for msg in messages:
    sealed = ss.seal(msg, aad=f"msg_id={msg.id}")
    store_in_db(sealed)

# Key rotation
ss.rotate_key('k02', new_master_secret)
sealed_new = ss.seal(msg, aad="msg_id=123")  # Uses k02
References
Related AETHERMOORE Components:

RFC 5869: HKDF key derivation
FIPS 197: AES algorithm
